posts.name: J D o v e
posts.cooked:
I stumbled upon this and found it interesting:

Because Beaujolais is a wine without tannin, without structure, without real concentration and richness, it will not age. It will not be transformed into something of depth and complexity by being imprisoned in a bottle. It will merely get dreary. Some will tell you a fine Moulin-a-Vent will change into something resembling a Pinot Noir Burgundy if given age. It won’t. This is not to say the wines won’t keep; any wine of balance and fruit will last if stored correctly. But, it won’t improve. On the contrary, it will become a rather strange brew of indeterminate character, of academic interest only.

– Clive Coates from The Wines of France 1990

posts.name: Mike Francisco
posts.cooked: In the last year, the tasting group I am part of, tasted about a dozen older Cru Beaujolais blind. Many from the 90’s the rest scattered either side of that, the youngest 2005. Though the wines had developed along a similar paths as Nuits Pinot and to a lesser extent the Northern Rhone, I found them still distinctly Beaujolais. As a group we agreed that this was one of the most interesting, eye-opening and enjoyable events we had done in some time.

I’ve had bottles that were 20+/- years old that would argue this point…But as others have noted, if he’s talking about Nouveau then I might not be so quick to judge. However, I know people that I trust and they’ve had bottles from the 40’s that they said were an epiphany for them.

What William said. Next poor Clive would say Phillip banned it because it was an inferior grape. An old Gamay can be quite wonderful. They age very well and always have. A lot of wine from everywhere in the world was pretty bad in the 1970s and 1980s. Some was wonderful. But Beaujolais has a long history of making great wine and the statement is BS.

Maybe he was referring to the nouveau version, but that’s applicable to the nouveau wines from every wine-making region. Not all wines are meant to age and not all of those that are meant to age actually do age. But to dismiss an entire region with a long history of producing age-worthy wines is just flat out dumb.

But it has us talking. And a lot of wine writing falls into the same category.

I’m legitimately curious, and if I were to go get a few, what would you suggest are “must try” bojo to convert a non-believer?

Before doing that – can you name 3-4 wines that are your go-to wines? The stuff you drink regularly and love above all else? That will help a little…

I legitimately don’t have that. I drink a lot of champagne and sparkling wine, but drink many styles and producers from Roederer Estate to vintage Krug. I drink bordeaux and napa cab, probably 1:1.5 ratio. I drink oregon pinot and lighter style cali pinot. I drink northern rhones from various producers. I like burgundy a lot, but don’t own or buy that much due to knowledge and QPR limitations. I’ll happily drink barolo, brunello (of older and newer styles), and even super tuscans. I like rioja and ribera quite a bit.

Not trying to sidestep you, but my buying habits are all over the map. To me that’s the fun of wine. There is so much great stuff and it’s great because of its differences. The two things I’ve just scratched my head over, though, are still rose and bojo. There are other things I get but don’t like to drink, like basically all stickies, amarone, big spanish garnachas, and overdone aussie shiraz and grenache. But I’m mostly pretty open.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
I agree wholeheartedly with William and Greg. Beaujolais ages beautifully and Clive’s statement is flat out incorrect. Perhaps he has learned the errors of his ways by now, (relocating to the region for retirement), as he did write it a long time ago, but it was no more accurate in 1990 than it is today. His blanket statement about the region was a disservice to the top vignerons there of that era and seems likely to have been based on a dearth of experience and a snobbish view of Beaujolais. No need to make excuses for him saying that the region had less serious winegrowers back in the '70s and '80s than it does today, as the same was also empirically true for the Cote d’Or and I do not see him damning that region with the same blanket statement. Either Clive just did not (or does not) know Beaujolais or does not like it (which is okay, as for each his own tastes), but that should have no bearing on the accuracy of his statement and he should have been professional enough to not make it in the first place, but maybe he was in his cups.

I had the pleasure to share the 1964 Clos de la Roilette Fleurie with William last spring in Beaune and it was the best red wine we had that night, in what was a varied and quite serious lineup. I have drunk Moulin-a-Vent bottles from the 1945, 1947 and 1949 vintages that were just stellar, with all of these being served blind as ringers at big, Cote d’Or events. I was also with Dale when we drank those old vintages of Domaine des Billards St. Amour and can confirm his impressions- the best wines were truly outstanding and every vintage was at least quite good and had evolved nicely. Cru Beaujolais can age beautifully and the wines are very interesting and complex at these advanced ages. Whether one happens to like Gamay or not is a completely separate issue from whether or not Beaujolais can age gracefully, and we should not confuse the two in the wake of a sloppily-made assertion by Clive, without his supplying any bit of evidence to support his assertion. He probably did not know the right wines to drink back then (or now); I could very easily put together a lineup of C List old red Burgundies for you to taste blind that would emphatically “prove” to you that “Red Burgundy does not age”. It all depends on the quality of the growers one selects. We have to be a bit more intellectually sophisticated when it comes to statements such as Clive’s above and treat it with a bit of skepticism, unless he comes up with some evidence to support such a sweeping statement.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
I love reading old wine books. It’s amazing how much more sophisticated wine is as a hobby in 2018 than it was in 1990 – let alone 1950.

So you’re a pot stirrer.

It’s obviously a gross generalization but why wouldn’t Dove’s comment be true? Winemaking has improved, wine technology has improved, vineyard management has improved, information is more accessible, wines from substantially all regions are more available, data can be shared in real time, even weather records are more accurate. There are more folks participating in wine as a hobby now than previously which has also driven competition for market share in substantially all areas of wine, including books, blogs, and criticism. Given all of those things, it would be astoundingly difficult to make a good argument that wine was “more sophisticated as a hobby than it was in 1990 – let alone 1950.” I’d love to hear the counter-argument to that generalization.

I like reading old wine books too. They can be illuminating in some ways and it’s fun to see how thinking about certain regions has developed over time. But I think the post above is saying something other than intended?

Given all of those things, it would be astoundingly difficult to make a good argument that wine was “more sophisticated as a hobby than it was in 1990 – let alone 1950.” I’d love to hear the counter-argument to that generalization.

As for this:

Beaujolais ages beautifully and Clive’s statement is flat out incorrect.

I couldn’t agree more. And for those who haven’t had a good aged version, you’re missing out. If you like aged wine that is.

Nice of you to stop by John.

Though as a more general point (which is not intended to be exculpatory), it is quite cognitively demanding to simultaneously maintain a respect for the sense and empirical evidence behind the hierarchical classification of sites in Burgundy, an awareness of the system’s flaws, and a willingness to acknowledge when wines simply transcend it.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Does anyone here know if and how winemaking in Beaujolais changed from say pre-1970 wines compared to the last couple decades? And have an opinion about whether Chauvet’s / Lapierre’s influence will affect longevity?

I know many avid Beaujolais drinkers, who buy for their cellars but drink their wines relatively young. (This is discussed often on Wine Disorder.) And I tend to benefit from local NYC folks who open younger Beaujolais often — say up to 12 years old. But, I only really cellar and bury Coudert and Desvignes for my cellar. And an occasional Brun and Bouland. Oldest is Desvignes from 1999.

I wonder not that Beaujolais has the capacity to age well and drink well and improve with age — no doubt. Rather, I wonder how many producers now are actually making wine that will age well. I would think that reductive winemaking that has become disfavored among many in Beaujolais is important, but maybe there is empirical evidence to the contrary.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Does anyone here know if and how winemaking in Beaujolais changed from say pre-1970 wines compared to the last couple decades? And have an opinion about whether Chauvet’s / Lapierre’s influence will affect longevity?

I know many avid Beaujolais drinkers, who buy for their cellars but drink their wines relatively young. (This is discussed often on Wine Disorder.) And I tend to benefit from local NYC folks who open younger Beaujolais often — say up to 12 years old. But, I only really cellar and bury Coudert and Desvignes for my cellar. And an occasional Brun and Bouland. Oldest is Desvignes from 1999.

I wonder not that Beaujolais has the capacity to age well and drink well and improve with age — no doubt. Rather, I wonder how many producers now are actually making wine that will age well. I would think that reductive winemaking that has become disfavored among many in Beaujolais is important, but maybe there is empirical evidence to the contrary.

This could be a deep rabbit hole, but in brief, the tradition in Beaujolais was semi-carbonic maceration, i.e. whole clusters fermented with foot treading to crush some of the clusters and liberate a bit of juice. Maturation was in small used barrels, then in the late 19th and early 20th century in foudres (which were introduced from Alsace where they were used for beer, interestingly). In the 20th century, concrete and then stainless steel entered the picture. Farming head trained Gamay is quite labor intensive, so as soon as producers could afford it chemical farming and fertilizer were adopted pretty zealously. By the 1970s, folks started doing full carbonic maceration to make fruitier, easier-drinking wines (same thing happened with Loire Cabernet Franc), typically with selected yeasts and sterile filtration. In the last couple of decades, thermovinifcaiton has become the big thing, and lots of Beaujolais is made that way today.

But you can find everything, from “Burgundian” vindication of destemmed fruit with long macerations (e.g. JP Brun), to classic semi-carbonic (e.g. Lafarge-Vial, Coudert, Daniel Bouland, Desvignes), to full carbonic [you actually leave the tank’s tap open so no juicy collects] (e.g. Alex Foillard, Marc Delienne, Chapel etc etc). As for Chauvet’s influence, he was a microbiologist and used to bulk out any wine that didn’t turn out well - the late Marcel Lapierre did the same. Jean Foillard’s best vintages with very little or no SO2 are able to age for twenty years. But given the immediacy of the wines and the fact that they can develop erratically, I don’t think there’s a strong argument for keeping no-sulfur Beaujolais that long.

Trends that would have made your average Beaujolais less age-worthy from the 1970s on would have been shorter macerations and more processing of the finished wine (especially aggressive filtration), plus overcropping. Today, wines made by thermovinification don’t seem good candidates for sustained bottle age. But as John and others have said, this is a producer by producer question, and there are plenty of producers who kept on making serious and age worthy wines.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Will be interested in whatever general comments you can make about 16s and 17s here. I haven’t tasted widely, but the 16s I have tried so far are starting to persuade me that, for my tastes, it may be the best vintage since 2010.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Will be interested in whatever general comments you can make about 16s and 17s here. I haven’t tasted widely, but the 16s I have tried so far are starting to persuade me that, for my tastes, it may be the best vintage since 2010.

May as well post my summation, based on tasting 600 wines: 2016 is a good but heterogeneous vintage, with frost damage in early May followed by hail in late May and June. Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie and the northern parts of Morgon were the most severely affected. Hail is not destiny, and it was possible to produce superb wines from affected vineyards: witness Foillard’s terrific 2016 Morgon Côte du Py. But plenty of wines displayed firm, bitter tannins and a lack of energy. The vintage’s best offerings, of which there are plenty, are classically balanced, succulent examples of Beaujolais cut from the same cloth as vintages 2013 and 2014.

2017 is a warmer, sunnier vintage of very high quality, with some producers comparing it to a hypothetical blend of 2015 and 2016 and others looking back to 2011. The wines are richer and more textural than the 2016s, without attaining the extremes of over-ripeness and hard tannin that sometimes mark the drought-impacted 2015s. Hail was a factor once again, however, and again struck Morgon, Moulin-à-Vent and Fleurie with particular ferocity. What would otherwise have been a homogenously excellent vintage is thus somewhat more varied in complexion.

posts.cooked:
A friend hosted a dinner last year with Xavier Barbet, from Domaine des Billards in St. Amour. Tasted vintages back to 60s. Both '67 and '76 were really great (69 was good). I like old wines, but I wasn’t making any excuses- I don’t think anyone would find the wines from the 60s and 70s tired.

I tried a 2018 Dom. des Billards [St Amour] over the last couple of days, and even though young, I thought it was really good. That’s a cru I don’t see much in my market. It seemed darker, heftier than I would have expected from a bojo. (I noticed Hanes said the same thing basically in a recent note he had on this). The acid was in check for my palate; I had it plain without food or anything. I don’t age Beaujolais, but if by accident one ducked its normal fate to be consumed young, it seems like this would have the stuffing to keep a few years longer. For my palate an A- grade.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
For me, the hit rate with Beaujolais for twenty five year old is similar to Zinfandel, very, very low. And well below Burgundy’s less renowned appellations such as Auxey and Savigny.


Cellaring Beaujolais for twenty plus years is a fool’s errand. It is not meant to be aged, and while you may occasionally come across a decent bottle, the percentage is incredibly low. I buy older cellars, and there are often bottles of Beaujolais hidden and forgotten, and as they are basically unsellable, I open them. As I said, there is occasionally something decent, but I can’t say they have ever taken on the complexity of older Auxey and Savigny. Smart betting is to drink early.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think cellaring Beaujolais for twenty plus years is a fool’s errand. It is not meant to be aged, and while you may occasionally come across a decent bottle, the percentage is incredibly low. I buy older cellars, and there are often bottles of Beaujolais hidden and forgotten, and as they are basically unsellable, I open them. As I said, there is occasionally something decent, but I can’t say they have ever taken on the complexity of older Auxey and Savigny. Smart betting is to drink early.

As a gross generalization, I suppose that’s true enough. Don’t try to age Dubouef for twenty years. But Bouland, Desvignes, etc, no problem. And the track record of the wines of the 40s, 50s, and 60s proves that if today’s Beaujolais mostly doesn’t age, that because of how it’s made, not because of the region it comes from or the grape from which it’s made.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I think cellaring Beaujolais for twenty plus years is a fool’s errand. It is not meant to be aged, and while you may occasionally come across a decent bottle, the percentage is incredibly low. I buy older cellars, and there are often bottles of Beaujolais hidden and forgotten, and as they are basically unsellable, I open them. As I said, there is occasionally something decent, but I can’t say they have ever taken on the complexity of older Auxey and Savigny. Smart betting is to drink early.

As a gross generalization, I suppose that’s true enough. Don’t try to age Dubouef for twenty years. But Bouland, Desvignes, etc, no problem. And the track record of the wines of the 40s, 50s, and 60s proves that if today’s Beaujolais mostly doesn’t age, that because of how it’s made, not because of the region it comes from or the grape from which it’s made.

Do you think they will ever be as good as old Ecard and Pavelot Savignys? Or Lafouge Auxeys?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think cellaring Beaujolais for twenty plus years is a fool’s errand. It is not meant to be aged, and while you may occasionally come across a decent bottle, the percentage is incredibly low. I buy older cellars, and there are often bottles of Beaujolais hidden and forgotten, and as they are basically unsellable, I open them. As I said, there is occasionally something decent, but I can’t say they have ever taken on the complexity of older Auxey and Savigny. Smart betting is to drink early.

As a gross generalization, I suppose that’s true enough. Don’t try to age Dubouef for twenty years. But Bouland, Desvignes, etc, no problem. And the track record of the wines of the 40s, 50s, and 60s proves that if today’s Beaujolais mostly doesn’t age, that because of how it’s made, not because of the region it comes from or the grape from which it’s made.

Do you think they will ever be as good as old Ecard and Pavelot Savignys? Or Lafouge Auxeys?

Yes.

I have Moulin-à-Vent in my cellar from pre-1970s that is better than plenty of mature Burgundy from grander AOCs that Savigny or Auxey.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Clive also comes from a generation that loved tannin and grew up when winemaking changed very slowly.

As people have pointed out, if white wines can age so can Beaujolais.

But to me the point of Beaujolais is that it is a wine to be enjoyed in its youth for its fruit. The exceptions prove the rule. I’ve enjoyed the exceptions but I am not laying any Ch Thivin down in the cellar.Then again,I am not buying green bananas either.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Clive also comes from a generation that loved tannin and grew up when winemaking changed very slowly.

As people have pointed out, if white wines can age so can Beaujolais.

But to me the point of Beaujolais is that it is a wine to be enjoyed in its youth for its fruit. The exceptions prove the rule. I’ve enjoyed the exceptions but I am not laying any Ch Thivin down in the cellar.Then again,I am not buying green bananas either.

…but if you had bought a dozen cases of 1971 or 1964 back in the day, you would now be reaping the rewards. I have a 1971 Morgon in my cellar that tastes just like Jayer. I don’t know what went on or how it was done, but it really has that profile… it was a lot less expensive!

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
That’s just a stupid thing to have written. Just last week I drank a 1969 Moulin-à-Vent from Château des Jacques that held its own against some Côte de Nuits grands crus from the same vintage. Earlier this year, 1964 Clos de la Roilette was even better. When you can find them, old Beaujolais from the 40s, 50s and 60s can be fabulous. And it isn’t by chance.

There I thought I’d been bold holding some of mine for 15 years.

Apropos, I quote myself on this topic from earlier today on another WB Beaujolais thread, writing about Vissoux crus:

"My experience has been that these wines can be rich in fruit (plush) in youth (especially the Poncié, though not the Garants) but, like most very good cru Beaujolais (and other wines) they shut down, passing through a kind of garbagy-to-dumb phase, to emerge on the other side a very different wine: refined, even silky fruit and tannins, supported by palate-cleansing acidity.

The cru Beaujolais I’ve tasted in this mature stage are comparable in style and quality to some villages and premier cru Burgundies of my acquaintance, though different in flavors-aromas …

I can enjoy Beaujolais young and old; however, tasting a good cru that has undergone the transformation I’m describing is worthwhile for the experience alone - the change in character is really arresting, and the resulting wine can be of startlingly high quality."

In any event, Coates’s signature work, like Kramer’s before it, still stands as a valuable documentary reference on the Cote d’Or’s domaines and vineyards - although more and more out-of-date with respect to the former.

In a related vein, there’s a comical chapter in the 1999 edition of Parker’s Wine Buyer’s Guide, titled “The Dark Side of Wine,” in which he tells of a wealthy ‘prestige’ wine collector who takes Parker on a tour of his cellar, then has the gall to serve Parker a Beaujolais obviously well past its sell-by date, which he suffers through manfully, with stoic composure, during the meal (it was also printed as a stand-alone article in the Atlantic magazine, and you can pull it down from the internet, if interested).

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
For anyone who has read enough of Clive’s stuff, or who has been at an event where he is there in person, he is completely hierarchical. Gamay is the lowest rung on the ladder for him. It can never be better than that.

+1. I subscribed to The Vine back in the pre-Burghound, pre-Gilman, pre-anybody-but-Tanzer era till I finally realized he was indeed a hierarchist. Grand cru was always better than premier and so on. His historical summaries of domaines were of great value, tasting notes not so much, probably because, like the equally useless Rovani notes, he was a heavy smoker.

Having said all that, I still love his succinct and apt 1993 description of 1990 DRC wines that sent me down a very costly rabbit hole: “a cathedral full of archbishops in all their finery.”

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
William,

In the mid 70s I was paying $4 a bottle for 1972 Rene Mugneret Suchots. Same price for Ramonet whites. Too bad I drank it all. Roumier Clos de la Bussiere…also $4.

In 1977 Gerald Asher set me up with a visit to Ch Thivin…we retailed their wines for $5.79…expensive!!

fancy_title: Clive Coates on the age worthiness of Beaujolais
title: Clive Coates on the age worthiness of Beaujolais
